## Two-baseline Comparison

> Per audit §4.4.4 / §5.4: every variant is evaluated against two baselines —
> (1) the most recent prior wave (continuous chain) and (2) a freshly-rerun
> baseline (the prior variant re-executed under the current run's
> reproducibility receipt). If freshly-rerun differs from historical by more
> than the wave's MDE, the wave's verdict is NOT actionable — debug drift
> first.

{{TWO_BASELINE_BLOCK}}

## Judge Reliability (Tier 2)

> Per audit §4.3.4 Tier 2 / §5.5: 20% of cross-tenant pairs (≥3 pairs whichever
> larger) re-judged with A/B order swapped; agreement rate computed on the gate
> metric. Disagreement > 15% flags the wave as judge-unreliable.

{{TIER2_INTER_RATER_BLOCK}}

## Effect Sizes

> Cohen's d (continuous) or Cohen's h (proportions) with bootstrap CIs on the
> effect (audit §4.9.4 / §6 post-run checklist). Surface every field —
> hiding the descriptive-only flag erases the whole point of this section.

{{EFFECT_SIZES_BLOCK}}

## Limitations (audit §4.12 forbidden claims)

- **No iid-bootstrap claims on pair-derived metrics.** Pairs share documents;
  pair-iid resampling fabricates precision. Only the stratified clustered
  bootstrap above is decision-grade.
- **No "the wave moved cosine by X" claims when N_events < 3.** The
  descriptive-only floor is a hard rule, not a recommendation.
- **No causal claims for layered changes.** When prompt + persona + variant
  shift simultaneously, the wave can rank candidates but cannot attribute
  the effect to any one layer per the §4.7.5 attribution-risk warning.
- **No transferability claim across event types.** The bench is fixed;
  generalisation to other event archetypes is a separate question.
